objects.name: CyberNetSec.io
objects.description: CyberNetSec.io is a cybersecurity intelligence platform providing timely, accurate, deduplicated, and actionable threat intelligence for security professionals. The platform aggregates, deduplicates, and enriches threat data from diverse public sources, mapping all threats to MITRE ATT&CK and D3FEND frameworks with operationally-focused analysis, detection guidance, and mitigation recommendations. Operated by security professionals, for the security community.

objects.name: Jason Gomes
objects.description: Cybersecurity practitioner and founder of CyberNetSec.io with over 10 years of specialized experience in security operations, threat intelligence, incident response, and security automation. Expertise spans SOAR/XSOAR orchestration, threat intelligence platforms, SIEM/UEBA analytics, and cyber fusion centers.

objects.name: Unnamed Vidar Stealer Affiliate
objects.description: A financially motivated cybercrime affiliate distributing the Vidar information stealer and XMRig cryptominer, leveraging the Factory-v3 loader-as-a-service framework. The actor targets a global audience, primarily in the U.S. and European Union.
goals: Financial gain through information theft and cryptocurrency mining.

objects.name: User Execution: Malicious File
objects.description: The initial attack vector involves malvertising that lures victims searching for pirated software to download and manually execute a malicious loader, often packaged in a password-protected archive with a .bin extension.

objects.name: Code Signing
objects.description: The Go-based loaders were signed with a fabricated Authenticode certificate impersonating the legitimate German company JustWatch GmbH. The certificate was issued by a self-signed root CA created by the attackers to deceive users.

objects.name: DLL Side-Loading
objects.description: A malicious DLL named MpClient.dll, which exports functions from the legitimate Windows Defender file, is used to hijack the execution flow. When a legitimate Defender process loads the library, the malicious version is executed instead.

objects.name: Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion
objects.description: Attackers inflated loader binaries with hundreds of megabytes of null bytes, with one sample reaching 491 MB, to bypass automated analysis in sandboxes that often have file size limits.

objects.name: Impair Defenses
objects.description: The Vidar stealer payload performs an in-memory AMSI bypass by loading amsi.dll, finding the AmsiScanBuffer function, and overwriting its initial bytes to force an error return, effectively disabling AMSI for the process.

objects.name: Obfuscated Files or Information
objects.description: The strings `amsi.dll` and `AmsiScanBuffer` used in the AMSI bypass are XOR-obfuscated to evade static detection. Additionally, the loader binaries are inflated with null bytes to evade sandbox analysis.

objects.name: Resource Hijacking
objects.description: The XMRig cryptocurrency miner is deployed alongside Vidar to covertly use victim system resources for mining Monero, leading to degraded system performance and increased electricity costs.

objects.name: Vidar
objects.description: A well-known information stealer malware designed to steal sensitive data such as browser credentials, cookies, and cryptocurrency wallets. In this campaign, it employs an in-memory AMSI bypass.

objects.name: XMRig
objects.description: An open-source cryptocurrency miner deployed by the attackers to covertly use victim system resources for mining Monero.

objects.name: Factory-v3
objects.description: A Go-based loader-as-a-service (MaaS) framework used to build and distribute the initial malware payloads. It employs techniques like code signing abuse, DLL sideloading, and file inflation to evade detection.

objects.name: Go
objects.description: The Go programming language was used to build the Factory-v3 loaders, as evidenced by embedded build metadata in the binaries.

objects.name: Malvertising Infrastructure
objects.description: Infrastructure used to host malicious advertisements and redirect victims searching for pirated software to malicious download pages.

objects.name: Vidar C2 Infrastructure
objects.description: Command and control infrastructure used by the Vidar stealer to exfiltrate stolen data.

objects.name: XMRig Mining Pool Infrastructure
objects.description: Cryptocurrency mining pools that the XMRig miner connects to using the Stratum protocol.

objects.name: Vidar Core Payload Hash
objects.description: Detects the creation of the Vidar core payload identified by its SHA-256 hash.
objects.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: Vidar Core Payload SHA256 Hash
id: 2b8c1f9a-3d1e-4b7c-8a2d-9f8e7c6b5a4d
status: experimental
description: Detects the Vidar core payload file hash identified in the Unit 42 report.
references:
  - https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/vidar-stealer-xmrig-miner-campaign-analysis/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-07-08
tags:
  - attack.t1204.002
level: critical
logsource:
  category: file_event
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Hashes|contains: 'SHA256=7ed4a256e1d281cb4f194d13ff554fb280dafde0a67a18115ea038ea6c87d'
  condition: selection

objects.name: MpClient.dll Sideloading Attempt
objects.description: Detects the Windows Defender process loading MpClient.dll from a non-standard location, a technique used by the Vidar campaign's loader.
objects.pattern:
title: Potential MpClient.dll Sideloading
id: 8c1d9e0a-4b7c-4f8e-9a2d-3b1c8f9a7d6e
status: experimental
description: Detects the Windows Defender process (MsMpEng.exe) loading MpClient.dll from a non-standard system directory, which may indicate a DLL sideloading attack as seen in the Vidar campaign.
references:
  - https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/vidar-stealer-xmrig-miner-campaign-analysis/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-07-08
tags:
  - attack.t1574.002
level: high
logsource:
  category: image_load
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\MsMpEng.exe'
    ImageLoaded|endswith: '\MpClient.dll'
  filter:
    ImageLoaded|startswith:
      - 'C:\Program Files\Windows Defender\'
      - 'C:\Program Files (x86)\Windows Defender\'
      - 'C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Windows Defender\'
  condition: selection and not filter

objects.name: XMRig Stratum Protocol Traffic
objects.description: Detects network traffic characteristic of XMRig connecting to a Monero mining pool using the Stratum protocol.
objects.pattern:
title: XMRig Cryptocurrency Mining Traffic
id: 1f9a8c1d-7d6e-4b7c-8a2d-9f8e3b1c8f9a
status: experimental
description: Detects outbound network traffic using the Stratum protocol (stratum+tcp), which is characteristic of XMRig and other cryptocurrency miners.
references:
  - https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/vidar-stealer-xmrig-miner-campaign-analysis/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-07-08
tags:
  - attack.t1496
  - attack.command_and_control
level: medium
logsource:
  category: proxy
detection:
  selection:
    c-uri|startswith: 'stratum+tcp://'
  condition: selection

objects.name: Factory-v3 Loader PDB Path Artifact
objects.description: Detects a PDB path artifact within binaries that is characteristic of the Factory-v3 loader builder.
objects.pattern:
title: Factory-v3 Go Loader PDB Artifact
id: 5a4d2b8c-1f9a-4b7c-8a2d-9f8e7c6b3d1e
status: experimental
description: 'Detects files containing the PDB path string ''C:\\Users\\Administrator\\Desktop\\UpdateFactory'' associated with the Factory-v3 loader builder.'
references:
  - https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/vidar-stealer-xmrig-miner-campaign-analysis/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-07-08
tags:
  - attack.t1204.002
level: high
logsource:
  category: file_event
  product: windows
detection:
  keywords:
    - 'C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\UpdateFactory\compiler\'
  condition: keywords

objects.description: A well-known information stealer malware designed to steal sensitive data such as browser credentials, cookies, and cryptocurrency wallets. In this campaign, it employs an in-memory AMSI bypass.
An open-source cryptocurrency miner deployed by the attackers to covertly use victim system resources for mining Monero.

A Go-based loader-as-a-service (MaaS) framework used to build and distribute the initial malware payloads. It employs techniques like code signing abuse, DLL sideloading, and file inflation to evade detection.

A financially motivated cybercrime affiliate distributing the Vidar information stealer and XMRig cryptominer, leveraging the Factory-v3 loader-as-a-service framework. The actor targets a global audience, primarily in the U.S. and European Union.

A financially motivated cybercrime affiliate distributing the Vidar information stealer and XMRig cryptominer, leveraging the Factory-v3 loader-as-a-service framework. The actor targets a global audience, primarily in the U.S. and European Union.
A primary target group for the Vidar and XMRig campaign, often lured by malvertising for cracked software.
A primary target group for the Vidar and XMRig campaign.
A legitimate German company whose identity was impersonated on a fabricated Authenticode certificate used to sign malicious loaders.

The Go-based loaders were signed with a fabricated Authenticode certificate impersonating the legitimate German company JustWatch GmbH. The certificate was issued by a self-signed root CA created by the attackers to deceive users.

A malicious DLL named MpClient.dll, which exports functions from the legitimate Windows Defender file, is used to hijack the execution flow. When a legitimate Defender process loads the library, the malicious version is executed instead.
Attackers inflated loader binaries with hundreds of megabytes of null bytes, with one sample reaching 491 MB, to bypass automated analysis in sandboxes that often have file size limits.
A well-known information stealer malware designed to steal sensitive data such as browser credentials, cookies, and cryptocurrency wallets. In this campaign, it employs an in-memory AMSI bypass.
An open-source cryptocurrency miner deployed by the attackers to covertly use victim system resources for mining Monero.

The Vidar stealer payload performs an in-memory AMSI bypass by loading amsi.dll, finding the AmsiScanBuffer function, and overwriting its initial bytes to force an error return, effectively disabling AMSI for the process.

The strings `amsi.dll` and `AmsiScanBuffer` used in the AMSI bypass are XOR-obfuscated to evade static detection. Additionally, the loader binaries are inflated with null bytes to evade sandbox analysis.
The XMRig cryptocurrency miner is deployed alongside Vidar to covertly use victim system resources for mining Monero, leading to degraded system performance and increased electricity costs.
An open-source cryptocurrency miner deployed by the attackers to covertly use victim system resources for mining Monero.

An open-source cryptocurrency miner deployed by the attackers to covertly use victim system resources for mining Monero.

A Go-based loader-as-a-service (MaaS) framework used to build and distribute the initial malware payloads. It employs techniques like code signing abuse, DLL sideloading, and file inflation to evade detection.

objects.name: Vidar Stealer Campaign Uses Fake Code Signing, Go Loaders, and File Inflation to Evade Detection
